Genesis: Herein, as I sit in my study in London in this year of our Lord 1699, I contemplate the very Cradle of Creation. In this first Book of Moses, the Almighty Creator did speak into being the Heavens and the Earth, and out of the dust did form our first Parent, Adam, in His own glorious Image. Yet did man, through the subtlety of the Serpent, fall into the abyss of Original Sin, bringing death and a curse upon all his posterity. But lo! God did not leave us transportless, for He did straightway promise that the Seed of the woman should bruise the Serpent’s head. We trace the Covenant from Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, through the sorrowful history of Joseph, down into Egypt, wherein we see the preservation of the Church in its infancy.
Exodus: In this second Book of Moses, we behold the mighty power of the Lord of Hosts in parting the Red Sea and bringing His elect people out of the iron furnace of Egyptian bondage, under the conduct of Moses, that great prophet and type of our Savior. We tremblingly hear the sovereign Law of the Ten Commandments delivered upon Mount Sinai amidst fearful thunders, which Law doth condemn our sinful natures. Yet did the Lord in His rich mercy vouchsafe the pattern of the Tabernacle and the Ark of the Covenant, which are lively figures and shadows of that spiritual Tabernacle, even the Church of Christ, and His redeeming presence.
Leviticus: Though our modern citizens of London, who walk the clean-rebuilt streets after the late dreadful Fire, may find these Levitical laws of sacrifices, purifications, and the Day of Atonement to be strange and archaic, yet to the eye of faith they are a glorious treasure. This third Book of Moses containeth the sacred directory of the Priesthood, wherein every slain bullock and goat is a shadow and figure of that One, Perfect, and Eternal Sacrifice which our Blessed Lord Jesus did offer up once for all upon the Altar of the Cross, to purge our consciences from dead works.
Numbers: In this Book, we see the numbering of the tribes and their long, forty years’ wandering in the howling Wilderness of Sinai, where they did repeatedly rebel against the Lord and Moses, murmuring in their tents. It is a sorrowful mirror of our own prone hearts, which do so easily forget God's mercies. Yet herein we behold the lifting up of the Brazen Serpent upon a pole, that whosoever was bitten by the fiery serpents might look and live—a most remarkable and comforting figure of our Savior exalted upon the Cross, to whom we dying sinners must ever lift our eyes.
Deuteronomy: This fifth and last Book of the holy Law contains the last, most moving discourses of Moses, written as he drew nigh to his death on Mount Nebo. He repeateth the Law to the new generation of Israel, urging them to singular obedience, and placing before them life and death, blessing and cursing. He foretelleth that Great Prophet who should arise like unto him, whom we know to be the Lord Jesus. As a cleric who must daily exhort my own flock in London to holiness, I do find in these warm exhortations the very marrow of practical divinity.
Joshua: Herein the valiant Joshua, whose very name signifieth a Savior, leadeth the armies of Israel across the parted waters of Jordan into the Land of Promise, casting down the walls of Jericho by faith, and dividing the inheritance among the tribes. This is a glorious, triumphant history, shewing that though we walk through a world of spiritual adversaries, our heavenly Joshua will surely lead His Church to a perfect and eternal rest, crushing all the principalities of darkness beneath our feet.
Judges: A dark and lamentable chronicle of Israel’s repeated defection and backsliding, and of the sore oppressions they suffered under their heathen neighbors. Yet did the Lord raise up valiant Deliverers—Gideon, Samson, and others—saving them from their foes when they cried unto Him. In these days of civil peace, this book warningly teaches us the absolute necessity of lawful government and a godly magistracy, lest we fall again into the anarchy of our late Great Rebellion, when there was no King in Israel, and every man did that which was right in his own eyes.
Ruth: A most sweet and comfortable history from the Days of the Judges, wherein we see the singular devotion of Ruth, the virtuous Moabitess, who out of love for Naomi and the true God did leave her kindred and country. By Divine Providence, she was led to the field of Boaz, who did act as her kinsman-redeemer. From this holy union did spring David, and at last our Savior Himself; shewing that even in the darkest times, God's promise of the Messiah was preserved, and that we Gentiles are not excluded from His sovereign covenant of grace.
1 Samuel: This Book recordeth the weighty transition of Israel from the government of the Judges to that of the Kings. We behold the birth of Samuel in answer to Hannah's prayers, the tragic rejection of King Saul for his pride and disobedience, and the anointing of the youth David, a man after God's own heart. How my heart is stirred by David's victory over the giant Goliath, proving that the battles of the Lord are not won by sword or spear, but by the name of the Lord of Hosts!
2 Samuel: Herein we read of the glorious and undivided reign of King David over all Israel, his establishment of Jerusalem as the royal city, and his godly desire to build a house for the Ark of God. Yet this history is also a warnings-piece for our souls, for it records his grievous fall in the matter of Uriah, his deep, tearful repentance (as we sing in the Fifty-First Psalm), and the bitter troubles that arose in his own house, especially the rebellion of his beautiful son Absalom.
1 Kings: This Book beginneth with the golden peace and unparalleled wisdom of King Solomon, who did raise that magnificent and costly Temple in Jerusalem, which was the wonder of the ancient world. Yet, alas, it recordeth also his sad declension in his old age, being seduced by foreign wives to build high places for idols. Whereafter the kingdom was rent in twain, and we see the rise of that fiery prophet Elijah, who did withstand Ahab and the false prophets of Baal upon Mount Carmel.
2 Kings: A tragic record of the decline and final ruin of both kingdoms, Israel and Judah, by reason of their persistent idolatry and contempt of God's prophets. We admire the double portion of Elisha's spirit and his many miracles, and the godly reforms of young King Josiah. Yet, at last, the cup of their iniquity being full, Israel is carried captive by the Assyrians, and Judah by the Babylonians, their glorious Temple—like our old cathedral of Saint Paul before the Great Fire—being utterly laid in ashes.
1 Chronicles: In this book, we are presented first with a sacred chain of genealogies from Adam, shewing the unceasing care of Providence over the holy line from which the Messiah should spring. It then recounteth the reign of King David, focusing not on his private sins, but on his public ordering of the Church, the courses of the Levites, and the preparation of gold, silver, and timber for the building of that Temple which his son Solomon should erect.
2 Chronicles: This Book continueth the history of the house of David, starting with the dedication of Solomon’s Temple, where the glory of the Lord did fill the house. It focuses upon those godly kings of Judah—Asa, Jehoshaphat, Hezekiah, and Josiah—who did cast down the idols and restore the pure worship of the Lord. It teacheth us that the prosperity of any nation, and of this our own realm of England, dependeth entirely upon keeping the true Protestant religion uncorrupted.
Ezra: A joyful yet tearful history of the return of the Jewish exiles from their seventy years' Babylonian captivity, under the decree of King Cyrus, raised up by God's sovereign hand. We see the laying of the foundations of the second Temple, and Ezra the Scribe restoring the reading of the sacred Law and purging the people from pagan mixtures. It is a lively picture of the reformation of a decayed Church, such as our reformers did perform in England.
Nehemiah: The godly and valiant governor Nehemiah is here seen rebuilding the broken walls of Jerusalem, despite the mocks and threats of Sanballat and Tobiah. He did encourage the builders to work with a trowel in one hand and a sword in the other—a truly noble emblem for every Christian soldier. He did also reform the abuses of the Sabbath and the oppression of the poor, showing that physical safety and spiritual reformation must ever go hand in hand.
Esther: Though the name of God be not found written in any verse of this Book, yet is His finger visible in every line. We see the wicked plot of Haman to utterly destroy the Jewish nation, and the wonderful way in which Queen Esther and her uncle Mordecai were raised up by Divine Providence for such a time as this. In the end, Haman is hanged upon the very gallows he had prepared for Mordecai, shewing how the Lord doth snare the wicked in the work of their own hands.
Job: A most sublime, ancient, and divine poem concerning the patience of Job under the heaviest strokes of Satan's malice. He is stripped of his children, his health, and his great wealth, yet in his ashes he layout his faith, crying: 'I know that my Redeemer liveth!' In my ministerial visits to the sick and afflicted in our London parishes, I do ever bring this book to mind, shewing that the afflictions of the righteous are but trials of their faith, and that the Lord is very pitiful and of tender mercy.
Psalms: The holy and heavenly Treasury of the Church, containing those divine songs and prayers of King David and other saints. Herein are devotions suited for every estate of the soul: in sickness, in joy, in penitence, and in persecution. In our daily cathedral service in London, these Psalms do lift our hearts to heaven, full of sweet prophecies of our Savior’s passion, His resurrection, and His eternal kingdom. They are indeed the very anatomy of the Christian soul.
Proverbs: A rich storehouse of ethical maxims and heavenly directions, compiled by King Solomon for the guidance of our lives. In these coffeehouses and streets of London, where youth are tempted by loose wits, the deists, and the paths of the harlot, this book is a necessary beacon. It commendeth that true Wisdom which beginneth with the Fear of the Lord, and layeth down instructions for justice, sobriety, diligence, and the bridling of the tongue.
Ecclesiastes: The penitential sermon of Solomon in his old age, wherein, having tasted of all the pleasures, riches, and honors of this world, he pronounceth them all to be vanity of vanities, and vexation of spirit. There is nothing under the sun that can satisfy the immortal soul, which was made for God. He concludeth with this great truth, which I daily preach to my congregation: 'Fear God, and keep His commandments: for this is the whole duty of man.'
Song of Solomon: A most divine and mystical love song, which, though written in the warm language of earthly spouses, is to be understood spiritually and with a chaste mind. It is a celestial dialogue between Christ, the heavenly Bridegroom, and His beloved Spouse, the Church. In this book, we contemplate the unspeakable love, beauty, and mutual delight that existeth between our Savior and the souls of His elect, far exceeding all the transient loves of this world.
Isaiah: This is the chief and most glorious of the Prophets, whom we may well style the fifth Evangelist. For he foretold, with such clarity as if he had stood at the manger and the cross, the virgin birth of our Emmanuel, His wondrous miracles, His being wounded for our transgressions and bruised for our iniquities, and His resurrection from the dead. It is an incomparable treasure that confoundeth all the blasphemies of the Socinians and Jews.
Jeremiah: The weeping prophet of Anathoth, who did stand as an iron pillar against the kings and priests of Judah in her dying days. He did foresee the terrible Babylonian captivity and did weep night and day for the slain of his people. Yet he comforteth us with the prophecy of that New and Spiritual Covenant, written not upon tables of stone, but upon the fleshy tables of the heart, wherein our sins shall be remembered no more.
Lamentations: The mournful elegies of Jeremiah, wherein he weepeth over the ruins of Jerusalem, her burned temple, and her desolate streets. It is a patterns of deep, national contrition. As I read these lines, I cannot but look back to our own London in 1666, when our houses and churches lay in heaps, and remember that our public sins do draw down the heavy judgments of God, and that it is only of His mercies that we are not utterly consumed.
Ezekiel: The prophet Ezekiel, captive by the river of Chebar, beholds those high and terrible visions of the Chariot of the Cherubim, and the Glory of God departing from the temple. Yet he prophesieth the return of that Glory, the resurrection of the dry bones in the valley by the breath of the Holy Spirit, and the building of a glorious, mystical Temple, shewing that though the visible Church be laid low, yet God will surely restore her in His appointed time.
Daniel: This book containeth the history of Daniel and the three Hebrew children in the court of Babylon, who chose rather to be cast into the burning fiery furnace and the den of lions than to defile themselves with idols. It contains also those wonderful prophecies of the four great world-monarchies and the seventy weeks, which did point with mathematical certainty to the exact year of our Savior's death, exposing the blindness of all infidels.
Hosea: The prophet Hosea is commanded by the Lord to take a wife of whoredoms, to represent the spiritual adulteries of Israel in turning to idols. He mixtureth heavy threats of ruin with the sweetest promises of mercy, shewing that though we have backslidden and played the harlot against our Creator, yet His divine mercy is unsearchable, and He cryeth unto us: 'O Israel, thou hast destroyed thyself; but in Me is thine help.'
Joel: He foretelleth a terrible plague of locusts and a consuming drought as a figure of the Great Day of the Lord's judgment, calling upon the priests to weep between the porch and the altar, and to rent their hearts and not their garments. He then foretelleth that glorious outpouring of the Holy Ghost upon all flesh, which was fulfilled on the day of Pentecost, and the salvation of all who call upon the name of the Lord.
Amos: A plain herdsman of Tekoa, whom the Lord did send to denounce the luxury, pride, and oppression of the poor in the kingdom of Israel. He mocketh at their hypocritical sacrifices while they did sell the righteous for silver and the poor for a pair of shoes. He warneth us that God will not accept our outward church-pomps if we neglect justice, and that righteousness must run down as a mighty stream.
Obadiah: In this brief yet heavy prophecy, the Edomites are denounced for their unnatural pride and the cruel joy they took in the desolation of their brother Jacob. They did stand in the crossway to cut off those that did escape. The prophet declareth that as Edom hath done, so shall it be done unto them, while upon Mount Zion there shall be deliverance, and the kingdom shall be the Lord's.
Jonah: The marvelous history of Jonah, who, fleeing from his mission to heathen Nineveh, was overtaken by a tempest and swallowed by a great fish for three days and three nights. This was a direct and miraculous sign of our Savior’s burial and resurrection on the third day. His subsequent preaching in Nineveh and their general repentance shews that the mercy of God is not confined to the Jews, but is open to the penitent among the Gentiles.
Micah: This prophet denounceth the covetousness and oppression of the princes of Israel and Judah, but comforteth the faithful with the glorious prophecy that the Messiah, the Ruler of Israel, should be born in the little town of Bethlehem of Judah. He summeth up our Christian duty in those golden words: 'to do justly, and to love mercy, and to walk humbly with thy God.'
Nahum: A most lofty and poetic prophecy of the utter desolation and final ruin of Nineveh, the bloody city and capital of the Assyrian empire, which had long oppressed the people of God. It comforteth the Church, shewing that though the Lord be slow to anger and great in power, He is a stronghold in the day of trouble, and will not suffer the cruel oppressors of His people to stand.
Habakkuk: The prophet Habakkuk pleadeth with the Lord concerning the violence of the wicked and the coming of the Chaldeans to scourge Judah. He is answered that the vision is for an appointed time, and that 'the just shall live by his faith'—a sacred truth which lies at the very foundation of our Protestant religion. He concludeth with a most excellent song of praise, rejoicing in the Lord though the fig tree should not blossom.
Zephaniah: He denounceth the severe judgments of God upon Judah for her idolatry, her swearing by Malcham, and her conforming to the strange apparel of the heathen. Yet, in the end, he turneth his voice to sweet comfort, promising that the Lord will gather those that are sorrowful for the solemn assembly, and make Zion a name and a praise among all people of the earth.
Haggai: After the return from Babylon, the people neglected the rebuilding of the temple, choosing rather to build their own ceiled houses. Haggai reproveth their coldness, shewing that their droughts and poor crops were the punishments of their neglect of God’s house. He exhorteth them to be strong and build, promising that the glory of this latter house should exceed that of the former, which was fulfilled when Christ entered it.
Zechariah: A contemporary of Haggai, who did encourage the builders of the Temple with many deep and mysterious visions. He foretelleth with marvelous detail the humble entry of our Savior into Jerusalem, riding upon an ass, His betrayal for thirty pieces of silver, His being pierced by those of His own house, and the opening of a Fountain in Jerusalem for sin and for uncleanness.
Malachi: The last of the Old Testament prophets, who reproveth both priests and people for their corrupt sacrifices, their divorces, and their robbing God of tithes. He foretelleth the sending of John the Baptist in the spirit of Elijah to prepare the way, and the sudden coming of the Messiah, the Sun of Righteousness, with healing in His wings, to purge his Temple before the great and dreadful day of the Lord.
Matthew: The first of the Holy Gospels, wherein Saint Matthew, writing primarily for the Jews, proveth from the ancient genealogies and scriptures that Jesus is the true Messiah. He recordeth the divine Sermon on the Mount, many heavenly parables of the kingdom, and the full history of our Lord's suffering, death, and resurrection, concluding with the great commission to baptize all nations.
Mark: This Gospel, compiled by Saint Mark (who our ancient fathers say did write from the mouth of Saint Peter), is a swift, powerful, and active narrative of our Lord’s public ministry. It focuses primarily upon His miracles, His authority over unclean spirits, and His labor of love, shewing Him to be the mighty Son of God who came not to be ministered unto, but to minister.
Luke: Saint Luke, the beloved physician and companion of Saint Paul, peneth a most elegant, orderly, and complete history of our Savior's life, from His miraculous birth in the manger. He alone preserveth those sweet, incomparable parables of the Prodigal Son, the Good Samaritan, and the Pharisee and Publican. He showeth the gospel to be a light of salvation to the Gentiles and comforteth the poorest of men.
John: The Spiritual and Eagle-winged Gospel, penned by the Beloved Disciple who leaned upon Jesus' bosom. He soarers to the very heavens to declare the eternal divinity of the Word, who was with God and was God. He recordeth those deep, celestial discourses concerning the New Birth, the Living Water, and that sweet promise of the Comforter, proving that Jesus is the Way, the Truth, and the Life.
Acts: In this sacred history of the infancy of the Church, we behold the miraculous outpouring of the Holy Ghost on the Day of Pentecost, the bold preaching of Saint Peter, the conversion and travels of Saint Paul, and the planting of the Gospel among the Gentiles. It is a wonderful pattern for our own reformed Church, shewing that no storms of persecution can stop the progress of Christ's kingdom.
Romans: Saint Paul's masterwork of theology, wherein he systematically unfoldeth the great doctrine of Justification by Faith alone, without the works of the Law. He proveth both Jew and Gentile to be under original sin and guilt, and that our only righteousness is the imputed righteousness of Christ. He handleth the deep mysteries of Election and Predestination, and closeth with the duties of the Christian in the commonwealth.
1 Corinthians: An epistle of wholesome discipline and correction, written by Paul to the unruly church of Corinth. He rebuketh their divisions, their pride, the toleration of incest, and their disorders at the Lord’s Table. He highly commendeth the grace of Charity or Love, and delivereth that most glorious and majestic defense of the Resurrection of the body, which we read at our gravesides in the burial office.
2 Corinthians: Herein Saint Paul defendeth his apostolic authority against false teachers, opening his very heart to the Corinthians. He recounteth his manifold sufferings, his shipwreck, his scourging, and his thorn in the flesh, shewing that the strength of Christ is made perfect in our weakness, and that we have this treasure of the gospel in earthen vessels, that the glory may be of God.
Galatians: The great Bulwark of Christian Liberty against those Judaisers who sought to bring the Galatians under the yoke of the ceremonial law. Saint Paul vehemently declareth that if any man, or even an angel from heaven, preach another gospel than justification by faith in Christ alone, he is accursed. This is the very book wherewith our Protestant Reformers did break the chains of Popery.
Ephesians: A most high and heavenly epistle, wherein Saint Paul soarers into the mysteries of our eternal Election and Adoption before the foundation of the world. He unfoldeth the glorious union of Jew and Gentile in one Church, which is the body of Christ, and exhorteth us to walk worthy of our calling, putting on the whole Armor of God to fight against the Devil.
Philippians: Written from a Roman prison, this is an epistle of sweet, heavenly joy and affection. Paul exhorteth the Philippians to unity and humility, setting before them the great pattern of Christ, who humbled Himself to the death of the Cross. He declareth that for him to live is Christ, and to die is gain, teaching us to rejoice in the Lord alway, in whatsoever state we are.
Colossians: In this divine letter, Paul warneth the Colossians against the dangerous mixtures of heathen philosophy and Jewish ceremonies which did seek to rob Christ of His glory. He declareth Him to be the Image of the invisible God, the Creator of all things, in whom dwelleth all the fullness of the Godhead bodily, and who hath made peace through the blood of His Cross.
1 Thessalonians: A very warm and comfortable epistle to a young Church, wherein Paul praiseth their faith, love, and patience under tribulation. He highly comforteth them concerning those who have fallen asleep in Jesus, assuring them that at the last trump, the dead in Christ shall rise first, and we shall be caught up together in the clouds to meet the Lord.
2 Thessalonians: Paul writeth to correct a mistake among them concerning the immediate coming of the Day of Christ. He revealeth that there must first come a great Apostasy or falling away, and the manifestation of the Man of Sin, the Son of Perdition, who opposeth and exalteth himself above all that is called God—which we in England do know to be the Roman Papacy.
1 Timothy: A pastoral epistle containing Paul’s fatherly instructions to his son Timothy for the government of the Church. He outlines the holy qualifications of Bishops and Deacons, the ordering of public prayer, and the care of widows. He warneth against those who depart from the faith, and delivereth that great warning that the love of money is the root of all evil.
2 Timothy: Paul’s dying testament and charge to Timothy, written from his cold dungeon in Rome as he awaited the axe of Nero. He exhorteth him to be strong, to endure hardness as a good soldier of Jesus Christ, to preach the word in season and out of season, and to hold fast to the holy scriptures, which are able to make us wise unto salvation.
Titus: A pastoral epistle to Titus, whom Paul had left in Crete to set in order the things that were wanting and to ordain Elders in every city. He exhorteth him to preach sound doctrine, showing that the grace of God which bringeth salvation teacheth us to live soberly, righteously, and godly in this present evil world, looking for that blessed hope.
Philemon: A beautiful, short letter which is a perfect model of Christian courtesy and love. Paul intercedeth for Onesimus, a runaway slave who had robbed his master Philemon, but had been converted under Paul’s bonds. Paul entreateth Philemon to receive him back, no longer as a servant, but as a brother beloved, offering to repay whatever Onesimus did owe.
Hebrews: An incomparable, divine treatise, wherein the writer shews the infinite superiority of our Lord Jesus Christ over Angels, over Moses, and over the Levitical Priesthood. He demonstrates that the sacrifices of the old Law were but temporary shadows, and that Christ is our eternal High Priest, who hath entered the holy place once for all, having obtained eternal redemption for us.
James: A very practical and searching epistle, written to prevent the dangerous error of those who think a bare, historical faith can save them. Saint James proveth with great force that a true, justifying faith must needs bring forth the good fruits of a holy life, of bridleing the tongue, of visiting the fatherless, and that faith without works is dead, even as the body without the spirit.
1 Peter: An epistle full of heavenly consolation, written to the scattered and persecuted Christians of the first age. Peter exhorteth them to stand fast under the fiery trial of suffering, to walk in holiness, to fear God and honor the King, and to commit their souls to Him in well-doing, as unto a faithful Creator, remembering the sufferings of our Lord.
2 Peter: Saint Peter's dying letter, wherein he warneth the Church against those scoffing heretics and false teachers of the last days who deny the promise of Christ’s second coming. He declares the absolute certainty of the holy scriptures, and foretelleth that the day of the Lord will come as a thief in the night, wherein the elements shall melt with fervent heat.
1 John: A most spiritual and comforting epistle of love, penned by the aged Apostle John. He giveth us sure marks whereby we may know that we are passed from death unto life, chiefly our love to the brethren and our keeping of His commandments. He warneth against antichristian deceivers, and assureth us that 'God is love', and that His Son’s blood cleanseth us from all sin.
2 John: A brief and affectionate letter written by the Elder to a Christian Lady of renown and her children, rejoicing in their walking in the truth, and warning them not to receive into their houses or bid godspeed to those heretics who deny that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh.
3 John: A short letter to the beloved Gaius, whom the Apostle commendeth for his hospitality to the traveling preachers of the Gospel. He sharply rebuketh the proud and tyrannical Diotrephes, who loved to have the preeminence among them, prating against the Apostles with malicious words and casting the brethren out of the Church.
Jude: A short, fiery epistle, wherein the apostle Jude exhorteth the saints to contend earnestly for the faith which was once delivered to them. He painteth in dark colors the character and terrible doom of those filthy dreamers and mockers who have crept into the Church unawares, turning the grace of our God into lasciviousness.
Revelation: The sublime and dreadful Apocalypse of Saint John, written in his banishment upon the isle of Patmos. It contains those high and mysterious visions of the seven golden candlesticks, the four horsemen, the fall of mystical Babylon (which our Protestant divines have long identified with the Roman Papacy), the binding of the Dragon, and the final descent of the beautiful New Jerusalem, where all tears shall be wiped away.
